1: pesol lecha shney luchot avanim karishonim (psol lĕkā šĕnê-luḥôṯ ʾăbānîm kāriʾšōnîm): 'chisel out two stone tablets like the first ones' — the covenant renewal requires human preparation; Moses chisels, YHWH writes. The distinction preserves both human agency and divine authorship in the covenant-making process. Aron etz ('a wooden chest') — the first mention of the ark's construction in Deuteronomy; its purpose is to house the covenant document, making the covenant portable and central to Israel's movement.
2: ektov al-haluchot et-hadevarim asher hayu al-haluchot harishonim (wĕʾeḵtōb ʿal-halluḥôṯ ʾeṯ-haddĕbārîm ʾăšer hāyû ʿal-halluḥôṯ hāriʾšōnîm): 'I will write on the tablets the same words as were on the first ones' — the covenant is renewed without revision; grace restores without lowering the standard. The broken tablets represented covenant rupture; the new tablets represent covenant renewal on identical terms — not a new, easier arrangement but the same demanding covenant given again.
3: va'a'as aron atzei shittim (wāʾaʿaś ʾărôn ʿăṣê šiṭṭîm): 'I made an ark of acacia wood' — shittim (acacia) is the same wood specified in Exod 25:10 for the tabernacle ark; Moses makes a simpler version here. The narrative conflates or summarizes the tabernacle construction; the key point is that the ark precedes the tablets — covenant-storage is prepared before covenant-document is received. Preparation for receiving the word of God is itself a covenant act.
4: aseret ha-devarim (ʿăśereṯ haddĕbārîm): 'the ten words/commands' — the technical OT term for the Decalogue (used also at Exod 34:28, Deut 4:13); devarim (words/matters) rather than mitzvot (commandments) emphasizes their character as divine speech before they are legal demands. Written again by YHWH from the fire; the second giving of the Decalogue is an enacted grace — the destroyed covenant document replaced by the covenant-keeping God.
5: va'asem et-haluchot ba'aron (wāʾāśem ʾeṯ-halluḥôṯ bāʾārôn): 'I placed the tablets in the ark' — the ark as the covenant's home; the law at the center of the community's physical structure. The closing note — vayyihyu sham ka'asher tzivvani YHWH ('and there they remain, just as the LORD commanded me') — grounds continuing covenant faithfulness on the original divine command, not periodic human recommitment.
6: The travel notation records Aaron's death at Moserah (cf. Num 33:30-38 where Hor is the site; the discrepancy may reflect different naming traditions for the same or adjacent sites). Va'yekahein Eleazar beno tachtav ('Eleazar his son took over the priestly ministry'): the Aaronic priesthood continues through biological succession; the covenant institution survives the death of its founding figure. Death does not interrupt the divine program — it transfers to the next generation.
7: The travel list — Gudgodah (gudgodah) to Jotbathah (yotvathah), a land of flowing streams (eretz nachalei mayim) — provides geographical specificity that resists abstraction. The mention of water-rich Jotbathah after the wilderness narrative is a micro-movement from scarcity to provision; the promise-land motif appears in the travelogue itself.
8: hivdil YHWH et-shevet ha-Levi (hibdîl Yhwh ʾeṯ-šēbeṯ halēwî): 'the LORD set apart the tribe of Levi' — hivdil (to separate, set apart) is the vocabulary of holiness-creation; the Levites are made holy by divine designation, not natural superiority. Three functions enumerated: (1) carry the ark (laset et-aron berit YHWH); (2) stand before YHWH to minister (la'amed lifnei YHWH lesharto); (3) bless in his name (levarekh bishmov). The priestly function is simultaneously spatial (standing before), relational (serving), and verbal (blessing).
9: YHWH hu nachalato (Yhwh hûʾ naḥălāṯô): 'the LORD himself is his inheritance' — the Levitical principle: no land portion, YHWH instead. This is the highest possible exchange: finite territory given up for the infinite God. The principle is eschatological: Rev 21:3 ('they will be his people and God himself will be with them') generalizes the Levitical condition to all of the new creation people. In Christ, all believers become a royal priesthood (1 Pet 2:9) for whom God is the inheritance.
10: velo ava YHWH hashchetecha (wĕlōʾ-ʾābāh Yhwh hašḥîṯĕkā): 'the LORD was unwilling to destroy you' — the intercession's result; YHWH's will is changed by prayer. This is one of the Bible's most direct statements that intercessory prayer alters the divine course of action. The theological question (does prayer change God?) is answered here: yes, within the framework of covenant faithfulness. Moses's successful intercession is the OT type of Christ's ever-living intercession (Heb 7:25).
11: qum lekh le'neso'a ha'am (qûm lēk lĕnasoaʿ hāʿām): 'get up and lead the people on their journey' — the renewal enables mission; after intercession comes commission. The covenant forgiveness does not merely restore the status quo ante but recommissions Moses and Israel to the original purpose. Asher nishbati la'avotam latet lahem ('which I swore to give to their fathers') — the inheritance-promise is renewed; the failure did not cancel the intention.
12: mah YHWH Eloheicha sho'el me'imcha (mā Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā šōʾēl mēʿimmāk): 'what does the LORD your God ask of you?' — the great rhetorical question; the answer is the Deuteronomic summary of the covenant life. Five verbs: fear (liyre'ah), walk (lalechet), love (le'ahavah), serve (la'avod) with all your heart and soul, and keep (lishmor) the commandments. Micah 6:8 compresses this to three: justice, loyalty, humble walking. Jesus further compresses it to two (love God, love neighbor), calling these 'all the Law and the Prophets.'
13: letov lach (lĕṭôb lāk): 'for your good' — the covenant commandments are not arbitrary impositions but the path to human flourishing. The covenant law is characterized throughout Deuteronomy as gift before obligation: YHWH gives the commandments for your own good. Paul's statement in Romans 7:12 (the law is holy and the commandment is holy, righteous, and good) restores this positive characterization after his analysis of the law's limitations.
14: YHWH Eloheicha hashamayim ushmei hashamayim ha'aretz vekhol-asher bah (Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā haššāmayim ûšmê haššāmayim hāʾāreṣ wĕkol-ʾăšer-bāh): 'To the LORD your God belong the heavens, even the highest heavens, the earth and everything in it' — the universal sovereignty statement; shmei hashamayim ('the heavens of heavens') is Hebrew superlative: the uttermost sky. The paradox of the next verse: this cosmic God specifically chose Abraham's descendants among all peoples. Universal sovereignty and particular election are not in tension; they explain each other.
15: raq ba'avotecha chashaq YHWH le'ahavah otam (raq bāʾăbōṯêkā ḥāšaq Yhwh lĕʾahăbāh ʾōṯām): 'yet the LORD delighted in your fathers and loved them' — chashaq is a strong word: attachment, desire, binding affection; it appears only 11 times in the OT and always carries emotional intensity. The election love is prior (raq = 'only', 'nevertheless' — despite their smallness) and generational (the patriarchs chosen, their descendants chosen in them). This is sovereign grace: the object does not explain the choice.
16: umaltem et arlat levavechem (ûmaltĕṯem ʾeṯ ʿŏrlaṯ lĕbabĕkem): 'circumcise your hearts' — the inner circumcision command; the physical rite that marks covenant membership (Gen 17) points to the inner transformation it cannot produce. Arlah (foreskin/uncircumcision) applied to the heart means the same obstruction that physical uncircumcision represents: exclusion from the covenant community's inner life. Jer 4:4, 9:25-26 develop this; Romans 2:29 completes it: the true circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit. Deuteronomy 10:16 is the OT's clearest pointer to the new covenant necessity.
17: Elohei ha-elohim va'Adonei ha-adonim (ʾĕlōhê hāʾĕlōhîm waʾăḏōnê hāʾăḏōnîm): 'God of gods and Lord of lords' — the double superlative; the only place in the Hebrew Bible where both titles appear together. Revelation 17:14 and 19:16 apply them both to Christ: Lord of lords and King of kings. Ha-gadol ha-gibbor veha-nora ('the great, mighty, and awesome'): the Deuteronomic divine-attribute triad that becomes the liturgical formula of the Amidah prayer and Nehemiah 9:32.
18: oseh mishpat yatom ve'almanah (ʿōśeh mišpaṭ yāṯôm wĕʾalmānāh): 'he upholds justice for the fatherless and the widow' — the marginalized triad of Deuteronomy: the orphan, widow, and foreigner (ger). YHWH's own character is defined by this justice-work; his ethics of inclusion precede and ground Israel's. Oheiv ger ('loves the foreigner'): YHWH's love for the outsider makes the command to love the foreigner (v19) a participation in YHWH's own disposition, not merely a humanitarian policy.
19: va'ahavtem et-hager ki gerim heyitem be'eretz Mitzraim (waʾăhebtem ʾeṯ-hagger kî-gērîm hĕyîṯem bĕʾereṣ miṣrāyim): 'love the foreigner, for you were foreigners in Egypt' — the experiential basis for social ethics: Israel's own memory of marginalization generates the ethical obligation toward the marginalized. This is the double-movement of covenantal compassion: what YHWH did for you when you were powerless, you must enact for others who are now powerless. The Exodus is not only a theological event but an ethical resource.
20: u'vo tidbaq (ûbô ṯiḏbāq): 'hold fast to him' — daveq (to cling, cleave) is the same root as Genesis 2:24's ve-davaq be'ishto ('he shall hold fast to his wife') — the marital-union vocabulary applied to the YHWH-Israel relationship. Devequt (cleaving/attachment) becomes a central category in Jewish mysticism; here it is the covenant's relational core: not merely obeying commands but cleaving to the person who gives them. Deut 10:20 is the OT ground for Paul's joined to the Lord language (1 Cor 6:17).
21: hu tehillatecha vehu Eloheicha (hûʾ ṯĕhillāṯĕkā wĕhûʾ ʾĕlōhêkā): 'he is your praise and he is your God' — the double identification: YHWH is both the content of Israel's praise and Israel's God. The equation of God-ness and praiseworthiness is the confession that the being who deserves praise is the one who acts for you. Asher asah ittecha et-hagedolot ve'et-hanora'ot ha'eleh asher ra'u eineicha ('who has done these great and awe-inspiring things your own eyes have seen'): the eyewitness element grounds the praise in history, not abstraction.
22: bishiv'im nefesh yardu avoteicha Mitzraimah (bĕšibʿîm nep̄eš yārĕḏû ʾăbōṯêkā miṣrāymāh): 'your fathers went down to Egypt as seventy souls' — the historical baseline (Exod 1:5, Gen 46:27); from seventy to as numerous as the stars (kekochvei hashamayim larov) is the Abrahamic promise fulfilled (Gen 15:5, 22:17). The numerical explosion across centuries is YHWH's demonstration that his covenant promises are not rhetorical: he counted seventy and made them millions. The God who counts the stars (Ps 147:4) counts and multiplies his covenant people.
